title: Variance Analysis
description: Test your understanding of variance analysis, favorable and unfavorable variances, price and quantity variances, and how managers use variance analysis to improve business performance.

questions.question: What is a variance?
options: The difference between actual and budgeted performance
The total amount of profit earned
A company's annual cash balance
The difference between assets and liabilities
questions.explanation: A variance is the difference between an actual result and its corresponding budgeted amount.

questions.question: Which statement best describes a favorable variance?
options: It improves profitability compared with the budget
It always means costs increased
It indicates that sales were lower than expected
It means the budget contained errors
questions.explanation: A favorable variance improves the company's financial performance relative to the budget.

questions.question: Which of the following is normally considered an unfavorable variance?
options: Actual maintenance costs are higher than budgeted
Actual sales revenue exceeds the budget
Actual material usage is lower than expected
Actual labor costs are below budget
questions.explanation: Higher-than-budgeted costs reduce profitability and are therefore unfavorable.

questions.question: Why do managers perform variance analysis?
options: To understand why actual performance differs from the budget
To replace the budgeting process
To eliminate all business risks
To prepare financial statements
questions.explanation: Variance analysis helps managers identify causes of performance differences and improve future decisions.

questions.question: A company budgeted sales of $500,000 but achieved sales of $560,000. What type of variance occurred?
options: Favorable revenue variance
Unfavorable cost variance
Favorable quantity variance
Unfavorable labor variance
questions.explanation: Higher revenue than expected is a favorable revenue variance.

questions.question: A company expected to pay $15 per unit of raw material but actually paid $17 per unit. Which type of variance is this?
options: Price variance
Quantity variance
Sales variance
Profit variance
questions.explanation: Price variance measures the effect of paying a different price than expected.

questions.question: What does a quantity (efficiency) variance measure?
options: Whether more or fewer resources were used than planned
Whether products were sold at different prices
Changes in exchange rates
Differences in company profits
questions.explanation: Quantity variances measure differences in resource usage, such as labor hours or material consumption.

questions.question: A factory budgeted 800 labor hours but actually used 900 labor hours to produce the same output. What type of variance is most likely present?
options: An unfavorable labor efficiency variance
A favorable price variance
A favorable sales variance
A favorable material variance
questions.explanation: Using more labor hours than planned indicates lower efficiency and creates an unfavorable quantity variance.

questions.question: A company budgeted maintenance expenses of $120,000 but actually spent $105,000 without reducing maintenance quality. How should this variance be classified?
options: Favorable variance
Unfavorable variance
Price variance only
Revenue variance
questions.explanation: Lower costs than budgeted, while maintaining quality, improve profitability and are favorable.

questions.question: A manager notices that actual fuel expenses are much higher than budgeted. Before taking action, what should the manager do first?
options: Investigate whether the variance was caused by higher fuel prices, greater fuel consumption or another factor
Reduce next month's fuel budget immediately
Assume employees wasted fuel
Ignore the variance because fuel prices always change
questions.explanation: Managers should identify the root cause of a variance before deciding on corrective actions.

questions.question: What is the main purpose of management by exception?
options: To focus management attention on significant variances
To investigate every variance regardless of size
To eliminate all unfavorable variances
To prepare financial statements more quickly
questions.explanation: Management by exception focuses on the most significant variances, allowing managers to spend their time where it has the greatest impact.

questions.question: A company budgeted to purchase steel at $500 per ton but actually paid $550 per ton. However, the quantity purchased matched the budget exactly. Which type of variance occurred?
options: An unfavorable material price variance
A favorable material quantity variance
An unfavorable sales variance
A favorable labor variance
questions.explanation: The quantity stayed the same, but the purchase price increased, creating an unfavorable price variance.

questions.question: A production department used fewer raw materials than budgeted while maintaining the same product quality. How should this variance generally be viewed?
options: As a favorable quantity variance
As an unfavorable price variance
As a favorable sales variance
As an unfavorable revenue variance
questions.explanation: Using fewer resources than expected without reducing quality indicates greater operational efficiency.

questions.question: A company's sales revenue was below budget, even though the selling price remained exactly as planned. Which factor most likely caused the variance?
options: Lower sales volume than expected
Higher material costs
Lower labor efficiency
Higher depreciation expense
questions.explanation: If prices did not change, lower revenue is most likely caused by selling fewer units.

questions.question: Why should managers separate price variances from quantity variances?
options: Because they often have different causes and require different corrective actions
Because accounting standards require two separate financial statements
Because quantity variances only occur in manufacturing companies
Because price variances are always favorable
questions.explanation: Different variances often originate from different business activities, making separate analysis more useful.

questions.question: A company's labor costs exceeded the budget. Further analysis shows that hourly wages matched the budget, but employees worked significantly more hours than planned. What is the most likely conclusion?
options: The unfavorable variance was caused primarily by lower labor efficiency
The unfavorable variance was caused by higher wage rates
The variance resulted from increased selling prices
The company experienced a favorable quantity variance
questions.explanation: Since wage rates remained unchanged, the additional labor hours created the unfavorable efficiency variance.

questions.question: A company reports a favorable maintenance cost variance. Before celebrating, what should management verify?
options: Whether maintenance was simply postponed, creating future repair risks
Whether depreciation increased
Whether sales prices decreased
Whether taxes were calculated correctly
questions.explanation: Lower maintenance costs are not always positive if essential work has been delayed.

questions.question: Why might an unfavorable variance not necessarily indicate poor management performance?
options: Because it may have been caused by external factors outside management's control
Because unfavorable variances always improve long-term profitability
Because budgets are legally optional
Because managers should ignore unfavorable variances
questions.explanation: Inflation, supply disruptions and market conditions may create unfavorable variances even when managers perform well.

questions.question: A purchasing manager negotiated a lower price for raw materials than budgeted, but the cheaper materials caused significant production waste. What does this illustrate?
options: A favorable price variance can lead to an unfavorable quantity variance
Price variances and quantity variances are unrelated
Lower purchase prices always improve profitability
Material quality never affects production efficiency
questions.explanation: Buying cheaper materials may reduce purchase costs but increase waste, reducing overall profitability.

questions.question: Which statement best explains why variance analysis is valuable for managers?
options: It helps identify the underlying causes of performance differences so future decisions can be improved
It guarantees future budgets will always be accurate
It eliminates the need for budgeting
It measures company value without using financial statements
questions.explanation: Variance analysis is most valuable because it helps managers understand performance differences and improve future planning and operations.

questions.question: A company's labor costs are 15% above budget. Management discovers employees received exactly the planned hourly wage but production was interrupted by equipment failures. What is the most likely cause of the variance?
options: An unfavorable labor efficiency variance caused by lost productivity
An unfavorable labor rate variance
A favorable material price variance
A favorable sales volume variance
questions.explanation: Since wage rates matched the budget, the higher labor cost resulted from employees working more hours because of lower productivity.

questions.question: A shipping company notices that one vessel consistently reports favorable fuel cost variances compared with similar vessels. What should management do next?
options: Investigate whether the vessel's operating practices can be applied across the fleet
Reduce every vessel's fuel budget immediately
Ignore the result because favorable variances require no analysis
Increase the vessel's maintenance budget
questions.explanation: Favorable variances can reveal best practices that may improve performance across the organization.

questions.question: A company experiences a large unfavorable material price variance due to a sudden global shortage of raw materials. Which conclusion is most appropriate?
options: The variance may not reflect poor purchasing performance because it resulted from external market conditions
The purchasing manager automatically performed poorly
The budget should never have included material costs
The variance should be ignored completely
questions.explanation: Managers must distinguish between controllable internal factors and uncontrollable external events.

questions.question: Why do experienced managers investigate both favorable and unfavorable variances?
options: Because favorable variances may reveal successful practices that can be repeated
Because only favorable variances affect profits
Because unfavorable variances are usually accounting mistakes
Because favorable variances eliminate future budgeting
questions.explanation: Both positive and negative variances provide valuable information that can improve future decision making.

questions.question: A department reports an unfavorable maintenance variance after replacing several worn-out machines before they failed. Which statement best describes this situation?
options: The variance may actually represent good management because it prevented more costly breakdowns later
Any unfavorable variance automatically indicates poor performance
Maintenance should always stay below budget
The maintenance budget should be ignored
questions.explanation: Some unfavorable variances create long-term value by avoiding larger future costs.

questions.question: A vessel's repair expenses exceeded budget by $400,000. Investigation shows that a collision caused unexpected damage requiring emergency repairs. What is the most appropriate management response?
options: Understand the root cause before evaluating performance or changing future budgets
Reduce next year's repair budget
Ignore the variance because accidents happen
Assume the engineering crew performed poorly
questions.explanation: Managers should first determine whether the variance resulted from controllable or uncontrollable events before taking action.

questions.question: A company only investigates variances greater than $100,000. What management principle is it applying?
options: Management by exception
Contribution margin analysis
Capital budgeting
Incremental costing
questions.explanation: Management by exception focuses attention on the most significant deviations from plan.

questions.question: What is the greatest risk of focusing only on the numerical size of a variance?
options: Managers may overlook the underlying business reasons behind the variance
Financial statements become inaccurate
Budgets automatically become flexible
Sales forecasts become unnecessary
questions.explanation: Understanding why a variance occurred is often more important than simply measuring its size.

questions.question: Which statement best summarizes the role of variance analysis in managerial accounting?
options: It compares planned and actual performance to support better operational and strategic decisions
It replaces budgeting entirely
It guarantees that future budgets will be accurate
It measures only company profitability
questions.explanation: Variance analysis helps managers learn from differences between planned and actual results, improving future planning and decision making.

questions.question: After completing a variance analysis, what should management ideally do next?
options: Implement corrective actions where appropriate and use the findings to improve future budgets
Delete the budget because it was inaccurate
Ignore favorable variances and only report unfavorable ones
Prepare next year's financial statements
questions.explanation: Variance analysis is valuable only when managers use the insights to improve operations, planning and future budgeting.
